articles.headline: Dig for on-line gold
articles.full_text: Time was when backgrounding a story subject wasn't for the fainthearted. It meant leafing through sheaves of yellowed clips, poring through dusty volumes, and reviewing sheaves of public records. Only the high-powered investigator with all the time in the world would attempt to deeply background a subject; the ordinary beat reporter working on daily deadlines didn't stand a chance. Even the privileged reporters who had the time to delve deeply into a subject's past wouldn't go on-line. Resources such as Lexis/Nexis, Dialog, Datatimes, and Dow Jones News Service were too expensive and not available to mere reporters. The explosion of the Internet and other on-line services has changed all that. Indeed, much of the information that once was available only by combing through books and dusty paper records or by paying hundreds of dollars for high-priced databases now can be obtained for no more than the $20 monthly it costs to connect with a local Internet provider or the $9.95 per month subscription to a commercial on-line service such as CompuServe or America On-line. Clips, Lexis/Nexis, Dialog and hard copy volumes such as "Who's Who in [fill in the blank]" are still invaluable resources that should be used if they are available. But journalists who want to mine all there is to know about their subjects - or even get basic information on deadline - do well to expand their horizons beyond the stalwarts. All it takes is a couple of on-line connections, ordinary journalistic curiosity, and a sense of adventure. Assuming you already have a computer, a modem running at a speed no slower than 28.8 kilobytes per second, a phone line and communication software to let them all talk to one another, you also need: An Internet account that provides access to the World Wide Web. This should cost about $20 monthly through either a national or local Internet Service Provider (ISP) and will let you surf the Web for as many hours as you like, using a Web browser such as Netscape Navigator or Microsoft Explorer. An account with a commercial on-line service such as America Online or CompuServe. America Online recently changed its price to $19.95 for unlimited access, mirroring most ISPs. CompuServe costs $9.95 monthly, including five hours of access, with additional hours at $2.95. CompuServe, especially, has deep research databases and is generally regarded a superior research tool. Either service also provides access to the World Wide Web, but the connections offered by ISPs are often faster and more stable. An account with a private data broker such as Database Technologies, CDB Infotek or IRSC, providing on-line access to specific information on individuals, their property holdings, etc. Some services can tell you what kind of car a prospect drives, how much his home and other property is worth, whether she owns an airplane, and more. The World Wide Web has achieved near divine status in the pop-culture consciousness. And the Web likely is the greatest (though least organized) collection of information known to humankind. It's all there wheat, chaff, and in between - in amounts that rapidly become overwhelming. The trick is to use the Web well, to learn how to focus your Web surfing so that you quickly get close to what you want. There is a myth that the World Wide Web is the Internet, but it's not. The Internet is simply a worldwide network of interconnected computer networks. Strip away all the hype and the Web is merely a means to navigate the Internet by way of an easy-to-use "point and click" interface. All you really need to know is that you use a "browser" such as Netscape or Microsoft Explorer to move around the Web and that a Web address looks something like this: http://www.nicar.org/nashcar Known as a "Universal Resource Locator" or "URL," this address directs the Web browser to open a particular Web page; all Web pages have an address that resembles it. Knowing a little bit more about Internet addresses can help a lot. For example the ".org" in the above address signals that the page is located on a computer connected to a nonprofit organization. The address would end in ".edu" if the site were associated with an educational institution; ".gov" if it's an official government site; ".mil" if it's military; ".net," if the page is on a computer attached to an ISP; and ".com" if it's associated with a business. This "domain hierarchy" can help you evaluate the quality of the information provided. What can you find? More than you possibly could imagine. Web sites will help you locate prospects' addresses and telephone numbers, articles written about them, and the socioeconomics of the neighborhoods where they live. In general, data that can be sold will not be available for free on the Internet. That means that Lexis/Nexises of the world are not likely to go away. Neither are the America Onlines and CompuServes of the world, where much of the same information is available at lower cost. Among them are: Dun & Bradstreet and TRW business profiles. Knowledge Index, a subset of Dialog containing more than 100 databases, including many major regional and national newspapers and many professional journals. The Biography Research Center, providing access to the full range of Marquis Who's Who volumes and more. Magazines and newspapers. The Magazine Database searches 250 popular magazines; the Newspaper Archives accesses 55 daily newspapers. Private data brokers, who electronically compile public records from around the country, can be the mother lode for researchers wanting to find out everything about a prospect. One of the most popular data brokers is Database Technologies of Pompano Beach, Fla., which offers a service known as Autotrak. While DBT does not offer information as comprehensive as some of the others, the price is right: $1.50 per minute, with no sign-up or subscription fee. Other brokers, such as CDB Infotek or IRSC, can be more expensive and charge monthly access fees. But they offer more information, up to and including information gathered from state court systems. On-line research will involve a learning curve. Plan on investing some time and money before you get proficient. Don't throw your books and other resources away. The on-line world is just one more tool researchers have at their disposal. It doesn't replace other sources, it supplements them. Remember that no single on-line resource will provide everything there is to know about everybody and everything. Remember, too, that information gathered via modem is subject to the same sort of errors as information gathered on paper. Use what you find on-line as a road map, but verify everything.
articles.summary: This article explores how the Internet and commercial on-line services have revolutionized investigative reporting by making complex background research accessible and affordable.

articles.headline: On the Internet: Backgrounding sites
articles.full_text: Several sites provide useful starting points for reporters beginning to background people and businesses on-line. One of the best is maintained by Nora Paul, the Poynter Institute's ace news researcher: an on-line version of her book "Computer-Assisted Research," that can function as an on-line tutorial. The URL is: http://www.poynter.org/poynter/chome.html. What may surprise many journalists, though, is that there is a whole other profession whose members are as interested in backgrounding people as they are. These are "prospect researchers," the folks who help charities and other nonprofits figure out how much potential donors can afford to give by checking out property records, stock prices, biographies, demographics - records equally useful to journalists. Here are a few sites: The Internet Prospector, a terrific site maintained by Randy Bunny at the University of Wyoming: http://plains.uwyo.edu/~prospect. The Prospect Research Page, maintained by the University of Washington's David Lamb: http://weber.u.washington.edu/~dlamb/research.html. Butler University's Prospect Research Home Page, maintained by Martha T. Murphy: http://www.butler.edu/~mmurphy/index.htmlx. Finding & backgrounding people: Use both the Internet and private data vendors to find out everything from prospects' addresses and phone numbers to the vehicles they drive. Yahoo People Search: http://www.yahoo.com/search/people/. Switchboard: http://www.switchboard.com. Four11: http://www.four11.com. Who Where: http://www.whowhere.com. The American Medical Association Physician Locator: http://www.ama-assn.org/. Martindale-Hubbell Lawyer Locator: http://www.martindale.com. West's Legal Directory: http://www.wld.com. The Biography Research Center on CompuServe (GO BIOGRAPHY). Data brokers compile billions of public records on individuals. Three of the better known brokers are: CDB Infotek (800) 427-3747, Database Technologies (800) 279-7710, and IRSC (800) 640-4772. For business research: Hoovers Online: http://www.hoovers.com. Dun & Bradstreet and TRW business profiles on CompuServe. Security & Exchange Commission/EDGAR: http://www.sec.gov. Stock Quotes: http://www.stockmaster.com. For news outlets: American Journalism Review: http://www.ajr.org. Major outlets include The New York Times (http://www.nytimes.com), The Washington Post (http://www.washingtonpost.com), and CNN (http://www.cnn.com/). General information: U.S. Census Bureau (http://www.census.gov), The White House (http://www.whitehouse.gov), THOMAS: Congress On-line (http://thomas.loc.gov), CIA World Factbook (http://www.odci.gov/cia/publications/pubs.html), and MapQuest (http://www.mapquest.com). Mailing lists like PRSPCT-L (subscribe at listserv@bucknell.edu) and archives like DejaNews (http://www.dejanews.com) are also valuable for tracking what subjects post on-line.
articles.summary: A detailed guide and directory of web-based resources, search engines, and data brokers for journalists to use when backgrounding individuals and organizations.

articles.headline: Pushing the bridge limits
articles.full_text: It sounded like a no-brainer: 13-ton school buses crossing 3-, 4- and 5-ton bridges running over busy railroad tracks? Trucks weighing up to 40 tons specifically directed to cross an 11-ton bridge over the same railroad lines? Couldn't be? Could be. In fact, that - and other potential disasters - are just what Gannett Newspapers found in the northern New York City suburbs as part of a two-part series in November that stemmed from searches through a statewide database of bridge conditions. Among the possibilities for serious trouble found by the newspaper were these: Fully-loaded school buses, which weigh anywhere from 10 to 15 tons running every school day over at least six bridges with signs barring vehicles of more than 3, 4 or 5 tons. Large vehicles ranging from 6-ton delivery trucks to 15-ton fire trucks to 40-ton tractor-trailers using the same bridges as the buses. A sign posted by one village that directed industrial truck traffic such as cement mixers, tractor-trailers and garbage trucks weighing well over 20 tons when full over an 11-ton bridge. After the newspaper chain notified about 10 local busing and trucking companies, all vowed to immediately change their routes. But that didn't stop experts on bridge maintenance and construction from warning that the potential for crisis continued to loom across the state. The difficulties in dealing with the problem included the century-old age of some bridges, commercial traffic routes running over many of these spans, police manpower too limited to consistently enforce the weight limits and a professed lack of funds by a commuter railroad that owns many of the most endangered bridges. The story began with an idea to look at general bridge conditions contained in a complex ASCII, fixed-length database of about 100 heavily coded fields describing the latest bridge inspections and results, kept by the state transportation department. Initially, about 50 of the fields were imported into a database manager using Paradox for DOS. We used a fixed-length importer called FLIMPORT that allowed us to pick and choose which fields we wanted to look at. We began to notice that many of the bridges popping up in the weight-limit field ran through commercial areas. We pulled them out, sorted them by community and weight limit, then camped out by 15 bridges for about a half-hour each. It took only a few minutes of observation at 11 of the bridges to see that the limits were routinely ignored. Nearby residents and merchants said the situation had gone on for years and that they figured it was allowed. Meanwhile, school-bus officials in several communities, as well as businesses whose trucks were seen using the bridges, professed surprise and ignorance of the problem and immediately said they would tell their drivers to re-route themselves. A county bus official said he believed it was all right to run 15-ton buses over 10- and 11-ton bridges, but later changed his mind after a state bridge official strongly suggested otherwise. A follow-up story is planned in a few months to see what, if any, changes are really made.
articles.summary: A Gannett newspaper series utilized a state transportation database to reveal that heavy school buses and commercial trucks were routinely violating weight limits on aging bridges.

articles.headline: Find it on CD-ROM
articles.full_text: Driving from courthouse to courthouse to dig through documents or ordering various faxes for background information can be minimized today with the many resources available on CD-ROM. Here's a wonderful reference list for CD-ROMs: DirectPhone/SelectPhone: Two/five regional discs containing both 10 million business and 90 million residential listings. SelectPhone can be searched by name, address, or telephone number. 11 Million Businesses Phone Directory: One disc containing business listings. 1.1 Million Health & Medical Industry Reference Directory: Listing everything from physicians to health clubs. The North American Facsimile Book: Contains 150,000 fax numbers. Military Personnel/Base Locator: Contains more than 128,000 service people and civilian employees. Statistical Abstract of the United States: Corresponds to the government bestseller containing a plethora of statistics. Budget of the United States Government: Contains all parts of the budget. Regional Economic Information System: Local area economic data including personal income and employment. Crime and Justice Data: Wealth of crime and justice data from the Bureau of Justice Statistics. School District Data Book on CD-ROM: Data derived from the 1990 Census data and the National Center for Education Statistics. Toxic Release Inventory for 1987-1990: Data about what plants are emitting what. Bureau of Transportation Statistics: Agency develops more than a dozen CD-ROMs a year including Traffic Safety Data. Mutual Funds on Disc: Analysis by Morningstar on 3,000 mutual funds. PharmAssist: Multimedia disc explaining drugs, legal and illegal. Marriage Records Series: Genealogical information covering marriages from various states. Geographic Names Information System: From the U.S. Geological Survey with about 2,000,000 place names. Federal News Service: Access to verbatim transcripts of Congressional hearings and briefings. U.S. Code: Laws of the United States. CIA World Fact Book: Profiles of countries. Military Contracts and Procurement Locator: Chronicles military contracts and purchasing. Defense Library on Disc: International affairs, science, defense, and military law. Street Atlas USA: Street level maps of the whole United States. Global Explorer: Great maps of the earth down to street level in cities. InfoTax CD: Nonprofit organizations summary data. Current Population Survey Utilities: Raw data from the annual survey conducted by the Census Bureau. National Election Studies: Database of basic research on voting and public opinion.
articles.summary: This article lists various CD-ROM resources available to journalists for backgrounding people, researching government budgets, analyzing crime statistics, and mapping locations.

articles.headline: NICAR and IRE Notes
articles.full_text: As the new year starts, we want to share news on the latest happenings at NICAR and Investigative Reporters & Editors. Sarah Cohen, recently of The St. Petersburg Times, becomes NICAR's newest training director. Sarah starts her new job this month and already is scheduled to travel to California and Pennsylvania. Sarah will be telecommuting from her home in Maryland. At The St. Petersburg Times, Sarah worked as a business reporter using computer-assisted reporting extensively. The schedule for panels at NashCAR, March 6-9, in Nashville, Tenn. is included in this issue. Among the panelists and instructors will be CAR guru Philip Meyer, Shawn McIntosh, Jennifer LaFleur, Paul Adrian, Neill Borowski, David Milliron, Bill Casey, Brad Goldstein, Bob Port, Tom Gaumer, and a host of others. At this point, the computer situation in NashCAR looks awesome. Vanderbilt University has generously loaned several computer labs containing about 140 computers with Internet connections. If you use NICAR services, then becoming an IRE member can save you or your news organization more money than ever. Uplink will cost $40 for members, instead of $60 for nonmembers. Our Web site continues to expand and improve. We hope to have new designs for our pages up and running in mid-February. Wallace Winfrey, our Web administrator, and other staff members are hard at working putting more handouts and other educational material on the site.
articles.summary: NICAR announces the hiring of Sarah Cohen as the new training director and provides details on the upcoming NashCAR conference and improvements to the organization's website.

articles.headline: Use FoxPro to document a table
articles.full_text: One of the paper essentials in computer-assisted reporting is the record layout. However, after you have created a table in FoxPro, it is very easy to document its structure, add in the data dictionary and even include narrative explanations of fields and stuff, all in one single file. You can do this by creating a structure description table from the data table and adding fields and information to it. Here's how to do it. First, with the desired data table open, use this FoxPro command to create the structure table: copy structure extended to NewTable. The table, NewTable, contains fields like Field_name, Field_len, Field_type, and Field_dec. You also can add fields to NewTable to help further document the original table- just be sure to add fields to the end. The most useful field to add might be a description field of 30 or 40 characters, since the 10-character field name limit in FoxPro can require cryptic names. You can also create a memo field for longer comments. This keeps the structure table usable for the next step - creating a new table based on the structure table: create NewTableName from StructureTableName.
articles.summary: A technical tip explaining how to use FoxPro's 'copy structure extended' command to document database layouts and create data dictionaries.

articles.headline: Slips tell the story: Pawnshops draw criminals
articles.full_text: Fort Lauderdale has the third worst property crime rate in the nation and has more pawnshops than it does McDonald's. Is there a link? Police have been saying "yes" for years. We decided to check it out for ourselves. In July, the Sun-Sentinel made a request under Florida's Sunshine Law for every pawn slip generated in the city in 1995. There were nearly 70,000. About 32,000 had been entered into the police department's computer; another 38,000 had not. We hired temporary workers to enter the paper records into our own PCs. Then we merged the two databases and loaded them into the computer program Paradox. The result was a gold mine of information: the names, addresses and birth dates of everyone who had pawned anything in 1995. We ranked the pawners by their number of trips to the pawnshop. Then we used local and state databases to check the criminal records of the top 50. The result: Thirty-nine out of 50 had criminal arrest records. We let readers decide if something was amiss when a 21-year-old convicted burglar goes to the pawnshop 66 times in one year. Readers were angry at what they read, and a state senator has already filed a bill to tighten restrictions on the industry. We also wrote a story about frequent pawners who were caught up in a cycle, focusing on pawnshops as "banks for the poor." If you're interested in a similar investigation, step one is to determine if pawn slips are public record in your city. Go in assuming you have the right to inspect them.
articles.summary: The Sun-Sentinel analyzed 70,000 pawn slips to identify links between pawnshops and frequent criminal activity, leading to legislative proposals for tighter industry restrictions.

articles.headline: NashCAR '97
articles.full_text: Learn more than 50 ways to use computers in the newsroom at NashCAR '97, March 6-9 in Nashville, Tenn. This year's National Computer-Assisted Reporting Conference is sponsored by IRE, NICAR and The Tennessean. Break-out sessions on special topics will be held in siderooms and speakers will give presentations on problem-solving, software, hardware, and data analysis in a demonstration room. Training will include classes in spreadsheets, database managers, mapping, nine-track tapes, creating World Wide Web pages, and surfing the Internet. There will be up to six hours of hands-on training offered to registrants on a first-come, first-served basis and one-on-one work critiques for the first 50. Panels include themes such as: Finding people and sources with on-line techniques; Using spreadsheets to cover local government; Making data accessible through Intranets; Data nightmares and how to avoid them; and Using CAR for international reporting. Sunday is a morning of workshops for database editors, news librarians, managers, and broadcasters.
articles.summary: The schedule and panel descriptions for the 1997 National Computer-Assisted Reporting Conference, featuring hands-on training and expert-led sessions on diverse CAR topics.

articles.headline: Bits, Bytes and Barks
articles.full_text: After our issue on Freedom of Information battles, Joe Carroll of the Hammond (Ind.) Times shared this story: The criminal court clerk in a nearby city refused to let me look at a court file for a woman jailed on battery and resisting arrest charges. The court clerk said she is, by law, the Keeper of Records, not the Distributor of Records. Eventually, it took a brief lawsuit to straighten them out. Jobs: The Miami (Fla.) Herald needs a capital bureau reporter skilled in CAR. The San Jose (Calif.) Mercury News needs technology reporters. The Orange County (Calif.) Register needs a technology reporter and an assistant city editor. The Wilmington (N.C.) Morning Star needs a court reporter.
articles.summary: A collection of short items including a story about an Indiana court clerk's refusal to release public records and multiple job listings for journalists with CAR skills.
